did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) and transfer learning with the Inception V3 model for skin lesion boundary extraction and classification. It also employs a color CNN approach for lesion boundary extraction and clustering-controlled entropy feature selection, evaluated on PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel automated approach for skin lesion detection and recognition using a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN). It details a specific methodology involving contrast enhancement, lesion boundary extraction, and feature fusion, and reports original experimental results with accuracy metrics (98.4% on PH2, 95.1% on ISBI 2016, 94.8% on ISBI 2017) on specific datasets, distinguishing it from a review.

title: Region Extraction and Classification of Skin Cancer: A Heterogeneous framework of Deep CNN Features Fusion and Reduction
brief_summary: This study proposes a heterogeneous deep learning framework for automated skin lesion boundary extraction and classification to address the limitations of manual dermatological diagnosis. The methodology employs fast local Laplacian filtering with HSV color transformation for contrast enhancement, a color CNN for lesion boundary extraction, and transfer learning using the Inception V3 model for feature extraction. Features are fused via Hamming distance and selected using a clustering-controlled entropy method before classification with a neural network. The system was evaluated on the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets for recognition tasks. The proposed method achieved accuracies of 98.4% on the PH2 dataset, 95.1% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 94.8% on the ISBI 2017 dataset. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms several existing state-of-the-art techniques in skin cancer detection and classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the most dangerous type of skin cancer. The study focuses on diagnosing melanoma in early stages. In the ISBI 2016 dataset, 517 images were labeled as melanoma. In the classification results (Table 7), 'Malignant' is used as the class label corresponding to melanoma.

Used as the comparator class to melanoma. The paper mentions classifying lesions into 'melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign' in the introduction. In the ISBI 2016 dataset, 2233 images were labeled as benign. In the classification results (Table 7), 'Benign' is used as the class label.

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the classes into which skin lesions are categorized in general ML designs ('melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign'), but the experimental evaluation on PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets appears to focus on a binary classification of benign vs. malignant (melanoma).

all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion classification into discrete classes: 'Benign' and 'Malignant' (melanoma). The primary task is binary classification using a Neural Network (NN) as the final classifier, achieving accuracies of 98.4% on PH2, 95.1% on ISBI 2016, and 94.8% on ISBI 2017 datasets. Other classifiers (SVM, KNN, DT, etc.) were also evaluated for this classification task.

The paper includes a lesion boundary extraction step using a color CNN approach and XOR operation, validated on ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets with segmentation accuracy metrics (e.g., 94.78% on ISIC 2017, 95.41% on PH2).

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy dataset consisting of 8-bit dermoscopic (DY) images with a resolution of 768 × 660. Ground truth images were provided by expert doctors.

use.training: Used for recognition phase validation; specific training split not reported for this dataset in the context (used for segmentation validation and recognition accuracy reporting).
use.validation: Used for segmentation validation (average accuracy 95.41%) and recognition phase validation (accuracy 98.4%).
use.testing: Used for segmentation validation and recognition phase validation.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image challenge dataset. The study utilized a subset of 1279 dermoscopic (DY) images for the training process and the remaining for testing.

use.training: 900 images utilized for the training process.
use.testing: 379 images utilized for the testing process.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image challenge dataset consisting of 2750 dermoscopic (DY) RGB images with resolutions ranging from 296-1456. The study utilized 2000 images for training and 750 for testing.

use.training: 2000 images utilized for the training process.
use.testing: 750 images utilized for the testing process.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy dataset. The study utilized 2000 annotated images provided by experts for segmentation evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To perform reliable and consistent recognition accuracy evaluation on the PH2 dataset.
To verify the recognition accuracy of the Neural Network classifier for the PH2 and ISBI 2016 datasets.
To present and verify the recognition accuracy and performance of the classifiers for the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 datasets.
To divide fused feature vectors into clusters for feature selection via clustering-controlled entropy.
To select the most discriminant features from the fused CNN features based on posterior probability and entropy values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-measure, AUC, and execution time, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

segmentation.notes: Overall average accuracy of 95.41% with an error rate of 4.59%. This is compared to a previous maximum of 95.30% [56].

recognition.notes: The Neural Network (NN) achieved the best accuracy. The confusion matrix showed 98% true positive for Benign and 98% true positive for Malignant (Note: Text states 'Benign 98% 2%' and 'Malignant 9% 91%' in Table 7 verification, but Table 4 lists NN Acc as 98.4%. The text summary confirms 98.4% accuracy).
The Neural Network (NN) achieved the best accuracy. Comparison with existing techniques shows an improvement over [8] which reported 94.9%.
The Neural Network (NN) achieved the best accuracy. Comparison with existing techniques shows an improvement over [67] which reported 93.6%.

segmentation.notes: Overall average accuracy of 94.78% with an error rate of 5.22%. This is compared to a previous maximum of 94.08% [56].

architecture_used.main_model: Heterogeneous framework combining a Color CNN for segmentation and Inception V3 (pre-trained DCNN) for feature extraction, followed by a Neural Network (MLP) classifier.
architecture_used.pretraining: Inception V3 model was pre-trained and then fine-tuned on the dermoscopy dataset. A VGG19 pre-trained CNN model was also utilized for the boundary extraction segmentation task.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported as a single fixed dimension for the entire pipeline; PH2 images were 768x660, ISBI 2017 images ranged from 296x296 to 1456x1456. The CNN segmentation model used a 2x2 pooling size.

architecture_details.segmentation_model: Color CNN (VGG19 based) for lesion boundary extraction.
architecture_details.feature_extraction_model: Inception V3 DCNN. Modifications included replacing 5x5 convolutions with 3x3, substituting nxn convolutions with 1x7 followed by 1x1, replacing last two GoogleNet modules with 1x3 and 3x1 in parallel, and modifying 7x7 convolutions to 3x3. Total of 42 learnable layers. Features extracted from Fully Connected (FC) and Average Pooling (AP) layers.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Hamming Distance (HD) based fusion of FC and AP layer features.
architecture_details.feature_selection: Clustering-controlled entropy (CcE) method using K-Means clustering to reduce features by 60%.
architecture_details.classifier: Neural Network (MLP) was the primary classifier, with comparisons made against SVM, KNN, Decision Tree, Discriminant Analysis, and Ensemble Bagged Tree.

training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy-like function for segmentation: Fξ = -1/N * Σ(yi,j * ln(p_wjξi,j) + (1-yi,j) * ln(1-p_wjξi,j)).
training_details.training_schedule: Fine-tuning on the dermoscopy dataset. Data split into 60% training and 40% testing for segmentation; 70% training and 30% testing for recognition phase.

classification_or_output_head.details: Classified fused and selected features into binary classes (Benign/Malignant). Achieved 98.4% accuracy on PH2, 95.1% on ISBI 2016, and 94.8% on ISBI 2017.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Rotation of images into 4 different angles (45°, 60°, 135°, 270°) during the training phase.
Contrast enhancement: Fast local Laplacian filtering (FlLpF) applied to input images.
Color transformation: HSV color transformation applied after FlLpF.
XOR operation: Applied between the enhanced HSV image and the FlLpF image for range selection of lesion pixels.
Thresholding: Otsu thresholding operation performed after activation function.
Morphological operations: Closing, filling, and area removal applied to refine the segmented image.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated into 4 different angles (45°, 60°, 135°, 270°) to address class imbalance. This process was performed only in the training phase.

conclusions: The proposed heterogeneous framework, which combines fast local Laplacian filtering, HSV color transformation, a color CNN for boundary extraction, and Inception V3 for feature extraction with clustering-controlled entropy selection, outperforms several existing state-of-the-art methods in skin cancer detection and classification.
The proposed method achieved a recognition accuracy of 98.4% on the PH2 dataset, 95.1% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 94.8% on the ISBI 2017 dataset.

Among the tested classifiers (including SVM, KNN, Decision Tree, and Ensemble Bagged Tree), the Neural Network (NN/MLP) consistently achieved the highest accuracy across all three recognition datasets.
The segmentation accuracy of the proposed method was 95.41% on the PH2 dataset and 94.78% on the ISIC 2017 dataset, surpassing previous methods (95.30% and 94.08%, respectively).
The clustering-controlled entropy (CcE) approach effectively reduced the feature dimensionality by 60% while selecting the most discriminant features for classification.

The study demonstrates that automated computer-aided diagnosis systems using deep learning can assist dermatologists by providing fast, cost-effective, and accurate detection of melanoma in early stages, addressing the limitations of manual interpretation.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets beyond the three public benchmarks (PH2, ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017), limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images) and exhibits class imbalance (40 melanoma vs. 160 benign), which may inflate performance metrics and reduce robustness.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods.
The paper does not report standard deviation or variance for accuracy metrics, preventing assessment of result stability or statistical significance.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of rotation-based augmentation (45°, 60°, 135°, 270°) without explicit confirmation that augmented images were strictly confined to the training fold during cross-validation.
The segmentation task was evaluated on ISIC 2017, but the recognition task was not evaluated on this dataset, creating a disconnect between segmentation and classification performance.

The proposed method relies on a cascaded pipeline (segmentation followed by classification); errors in the boundary extraction step may propagate and degrade final classification accuracy, though this error propagation is not quantified.

The study compares against older or less comprehensive baselines (e.g., handcrafted features) rather than recent deep learning architectures (e.g., U-Net, ResNet) that are standard in current dermatology AI research.

Execution times vary significantly across classifiers (e.g., 3.25s for NN vs. 139.43s for Cubic SVM on ISBI 2017), but computational efficiency is not normalized for hardware specifications, limiting reproducibility of deployment claims.
